1: mathētēs tis ēn ekei onomati Timotheos ('a certain disciple was there named Timothy'): Luke's first introduction of Timothy. Gynaikos Ioudaias pistēs ('son of a Jewish woman who was a believer') — Timothy's mixed parentage (Jewish mother, Greek father) creates the legal and pastoral problem of Acts 16:3. In Jewish law, Jewish identity follows the mother; Timothy was therefore technically Jewish but uncircumcised. He will become Paul's most significant co-worker (Philippians 2:19-22; 1 Corinthians 4:17) and the addressee of two NT letters.
2: emartyreito hypo tōn en Lystrois kai Ikonion adelphōn ('well-attested by the brothers at Lystra and Iconium'): the same martyreō vocabulary used for witness and testimony throughout Acts now describes Timothy's community reputation. The practice of commendation across churches — a written or oral recommendation from the home community — will become formalized in Paul's letters (2 Corinthians 3:1-3; Romans 16:1-2). Timothy's acceptability to the mission depends on the established community's verification.
3: perietemen auton dia tous Ioudaious ('he circumcised him because of the Jews'): Paul's circumcision of Timothy is not a theological contradiction of Galatians 2:3-5 (where Titus, a Gentile, was not compelled to be circumcised) but a pragmatic pastoral act. Timothy's mother was Jewish, making him legally Jewish; his uncircumcision was a social-religious disability for synagogue ministry. The circumcision facilitates access to the synagogue, not salvation. Paul's principle was 'to the Jews, I became as a Jew' (1 Corinthians 9:20).
4: ta dogmata ta kekriminena hypo tōn apostolōn kai presbyterōn ('the decisions reached by the apostles and elders'): the Jerusalem Council's rulings (Acts 15:29) are here transmitted in written form (paradidōsin phylassein, 'delivered for them to keep') to the Gentile churches. Dogma (decree/decision) in Acts is used for both Roman imperial decrees (Acts 17:7) and the apostolic council's ruling — a subtle leveling: the church's apostolic decisions carry the same force as imperial edicts, but from a higher authority.
5: hai men oun ekklēsiai estereunto tē pistei kai eperisseuon tō arithmō kath' hēmeran: another growth summary (cf. Acts 2:47; Acts 4:4; Acts 6:7; Acts 12:24; Acts 13:49). Steredō (strengthened/consolidated) is the same root as the healing of the lame man in Acts 3:7 — a community being made firm in faith. The delivery of the apostolic decrees produces consolidation, not fragmentation; the Council's rulings built up rather than divided.
6: kōlythentes hypo tou Hagiou Pneumatos lalēsai ton logon en tē Asia ('having been prevented by the Holy Spirit from speaking the word in Asia'): the Spirit's prohibition of a seemingly strategic mission field (the province of Asia, whose capital Ephesus would later become Paul's major base) illustrates Acts' theology of divine mission direction. The Spirit does not merely guide positively; it also prohibits. Asia Minor will receive the gospel extensively later (Acts 19:10); the Spirit's timing, not Paul's initiative, governs the sequence.
7: to pneuma Iēsou ouk eiasen autous ('the Spirit of Jesus did not allow them'): the only occurrence in Acts of 'the Spirit of Jesus' — Luke's most Christologically explicit pneumatology. The Spirit who guides the mission is identified directly with the risen Jesus; the post-Pentecost Spirit is Jesus's own presence and agency in the community (cf. John 14:16-18; 2 Corinthians 3:17). Paul's letters use 'Spirit of Christ' (Romans 8:9) and 'Spirit of Jesus Christ' (Philippians 1:19); Luke here uses the precise christological form.
8: katabantes eis Trōada ('having gone down to Troas'): the passive drift westward — prevented from going east into Asia (Acts 16:6), north into Bithynia (Acts 16:7), now at the Aegean port — positions Paul at the westernmost accessible point. The geography is providentially constructed: the Spirit closes every door except the one facing Europe. Troas was the ancient site of Troy; its association with East-West exchange makes it the fitting threshold for the European mission.
9: horama dia tēs nyktos ('a vision through the night'): Acts' directional visions occur at transition points in the mission (Acts 9:10-12; Acts 10:3, 10-16; Acts 16:9; Acts 18:9; Acts 22:17-21; Acts 23:11). Anēr Makedōn tis ēn ('a certain Macedonian man'): the figure's identity is unspecified — whether a person in need, a symbolic figure, or an angelic messenger is left open. The appeal — diabas eis Makedonian boēthēson hēmin ('come over to Macedonia and help us') — is the mission's commissioning for Europe.
10: eutheos ezētēsamen exelthein eis Makedonian ('we immediately sought to go to Macedonia'): the first-person plural 'we' marks the beginning of Luke's eyewitness travel narrative. Luke, the author, is apparently at Troas and joins the party here. Symbibasontes hoti proskeklētai hēmas ho theos euangelisasthai autous ('concluding that God had called us to proclaim the good news to them'): the mission team's theological interpretation of the vision — they reason that the vision is divine calling, not merely natural dreaming. Their conclusion-language (symbibazō) reflects deliberate discernment.
11: euthudromēsamen ('we ran a straight course'): nautical vocabulary for sailing directly downwind — the ancient equivalent of a fast, favorable passage. The detail (two-day voyage mentioned by implication) is characteristic of the 'we' passages' travel specificity; contrast the lengthier indirect journey in the reverse direction (Acts 20:6 — five days instead of two). The favorable wind of Acts 16:11 will become an unfavorable passage on the return; the difference in journey time marks the providential opening of the European mission.
12: Philippoi, hētis estin prōtē tēs merides Makedonias polis, kolōnia ('Philippi, which is the leading city of that district of Macedonia, a Roman colony'): Philippi's status as a kolōnia (Roman colony) is significant. Roman colonies were settlements of Roman citizens, governed by Roman law, with the rights and obligations of Roman citizens. They replicated Rome in miniature in the provinces. The legal and social dynamics of a colony — Roman citizenship, magistrates with full civic authority, privilege of Roman customs — will drive the narrative of Acts 16:19-40.
13: proseuche ('a place of prayer'): Jewish communities that lacked the ten adult males required to constitute a synagogue used outdoor prayer places, often near water for ritual washing. Luke's note that the missionaries 'expected to find' (enomizomen proseuchen einai) such a place shows their strategic intelligence about diaspora Jewish practice. Kathisantes elalomen tais synelthousais gynaixi ('sitting down we spoke to the women who had gathered') — the initial audience at Philippi is an exclusively female gathering.
14: Lydia onomati, porphyropōlis ('a woman named Lydia, a dealer in purple'): purple dye and purple-dyed cloth were luxury goods associated with wealth and power (purple = imperial color). Lydia's trade connected her to the aristocracy and the imperial system; she is economically significant, not a marginal figure. Sebomenē ton theon ('a worshipper of God') identifies her as a Gentile God-fearer. diēnoixen ho kyrios tēn kardian ('the Lord opened her heart'): divine causation of receptivity — faith is a gift of opened perception, not merely human decision.
15: ei kekrikate me pistēn tō kyriō einai, eiselthontes eis ton oikon mou menete ('if you have judged me to be faithful to the Lord, come into my house and stay'): Lydia's invitation is conditional on her hosts' assessment of her — not false humility but the social form of ancient hospitality. Her home becomes the base for the Philippian mission and the meeting place of the church (Acts 16:40). The first European church is hosted by a businesswoman; Luke's narrative attention to women throughout Acts-Luke is consistent and intentional.
16: pneuma python ('a python spirit'): in Greek religion, the Python was the serpent at Delphi through whom Apollo spoke via the oracle (Pythia). The term python identified divination spirits; the slave-girl's owners exploited her for financial gain. Her spirit's theological accuracy — 'servants of the Most High God, who tell you the way of salvation' — is ironic: a demonic spirit advertises the truth it serves to undermine. Paul's eventual exorcism removes the truth-proclamation the spirit was making, which provokes the commercial backlash of Acts 16:19.
17: houtoi hoi anthrōpoi douloi tou theou tou hypsistou eisin ('these men are servants of the Most High God'): the demon's acclamation identifies both Paul's mission (douloi, servants) and its source (theou tou hypsistou, God Most High). Theos hypsistos was a title used across the Greco-Roman world for a supreme deity — by Jews for YHWH, and by Gentiles for Zeus or a syncretistic supreme god. The slave-girl's cry could be heard in multiple registers; her proclamation is accurate but misleading about which 'Most High God' is meant.
18: paraggellō soi en onomati Iēsou Christou exelthein ('I command you in the name of Jesus Christ to come out'): the exorcism formula — authority invested in the name — is the consistent pattern of apostolic deliverance in Acts (Acts 3:6; Acts 4:10; Acts 16:18). Paul's eventual annoyance (diaponētheis, 'being thoroughly troubled/worn down') after 'many days' is psychologically realistic; the spirit's harassment was sustained, not instantaneous. The exorcism succeeds that very hour (tē autē hora) — Luke's precision on the immediacy of the Spirit's power.
19: idōntes hoi kyrioi autēs hoti exēlthen hē elpis tēs ergasias autōn ('her owners saw that their hope of profit was gone'): the economic motivation is named without apology — the exorcism's social consequence is financial loss. Epilabomenoi ton Paulon kai ton Silan ('seizing Paul and Silas'): the economic grievance produces the physical assault. Acts repeatedly shows that the gospel's disruption of exploitative economic systems (idolatry, divination, magic) generates the most violent responses. The persecution here is not religious but commercial.
20: Ioudaioi hyparchontes ('being Jews'): the charge before the magistrates deploys ethnic hostility as its rhetorical weapon. In the Roman world, being Jewish was not illegal, but promoting 'customs unlawful for Romans' was. The accusers weaponize Paul's Jewish identity to trigger Roman civic anxiety. Luke's recording of this charge documents the social dynamics of early Roman anti-Judaism and shows how the gospel mission navigated ethnic suspicion.
21: ethē ha ouk exestin hēmin paradechesthai oud' poiein Rhōmaiois ousin ('customs which it is not lawful for us who are Romans to accept or practice'): the charge is technically about illegal foreign religious promotion, but its real content is economic grievance dressed in civic rhetoric. Roman law protected traditional religious practices but was suspicious of new Oriental cults that disrupted the social order. The magistrates (strategoi, the two ruling magistrates of a Roman colony) respond to the crowd's excitement, not to a carefully weighed legal case.
22: rhabdouchoi ('lictors/rod-bearers'): the magistrates' officers who carried the fasces (bundle of rods symbolizing Roman authority to punish). The public flogging without trial (perirrēxantes autōn ta himatia, stripping off their garments; ekeleuon rhabdizein, ordered them beaten with rods) was the Roman punishment fustigatio. That this happened to Roman citizens without due process is the legal violation Paul will invoke in Acts 16:37.
23: pollas epithenthes autois plēgas ('inflicting many blows upon them'): Luke's understated vocabulary for a severe public beating. The subsequent imprisonment — desmophylaki (the prison) with orders for maximum security (Acts 16:24) — treats the missionaries as dangerous criminals requiring the most stringent custody. Paul will later summarize this experience as one of five Roman beatings received (2 Corinthians 11:23-25), placing it in the context of his catalog of apostolic suffering.
24: esō phylakēn ('inner cell') + ta xyla ('the stocks'): the inner cell was the most secure part of the prison, typically underground. The stocks were a wooden restraint locking the feet apart and preventing any movement — a device of immobilization and humiliation as much as security. The extreme security measures set up the earthquake miracle's dramatic contrast: maximum human restraint, effortlessly overturned by divine action.
25: kata de to mesonyktion Paulos kai Silas proseuchomenoi humnoun ton theon ('about midnight Paul and Silas were praying and singing hymns to God'): hymnousin — the only occurrence of this verb in Acts — describes liturgical song. The midnight prayer-and-song in a Roman prison cell has been read against the background of the Psalter (Psalms 119:62, 'At midnight I rise to give you thanks'), the prophetic tradition (Job 35:10, 'songs in the night'), and the Pauline instruction to sing (Ephesians 5:19; Colossians 3:16). The other prisoners' listening (epēkroōnto) is noted without further comment — their receptivity has been prepared by the missionaries' public testimony.
26: seismos megas ('a great earthquake') + eutheos de aneōchthēsan pasai ai thyrai ('immediately all the doors were opened'): the seismic theophany echoes the Sinai tradition (Exodus 19:18; Psalms 68:8) and the Pentecost wind (Acts 2:2). The earthquake's simultaneous effect on all doors and all chains is physically impossible in normal terms — not a coincidental earthquake but a directed divine intervention. Luke does not distinguish between natural earthquake and supernatural liberation; both are the same God acting.
27: mellōn heauton anairein ('about to kill himself'): the jailer's response to apparently empty cells is conditioned by Roman military law — a soldier who allowed a prisoner to escape faced the same punishment as the prisoner. Suicide was preferable to the degradation of execution for dereliction. Paul's cry from inside (mēden praxēs seautō kakon, 'do yourself no harm') stops him before he can act — Paul can see in the darkness what the jailer cannot. The scene's dramatic irony is that the prisoners' voluntary presence saves the jailer's life before he knows it.
28: Paul's shout — phone megalē (a loud voice) — penetrates the dark: 'Don't harm yourself! We are all here!' The unexpected words carry double weight: the prisoners have not fled (a miracle in itself given open doors) and the jailer's life is preserved. Luke does not explain why the prisoners did not flee; Paul's presence and presumably authority kept them. The scene reverses the normal prison dynamic: the prisoner saves the jailer.
29: aitēsas phōta eisepēdēsen ('having called for lights, he rushed in'): the jailer's sequence — requesting lights, rushing in, falling trembling before Paul and Silas (entromos genomenos prosepesen tō Paulō kai tō Silā) — shows progressive recognition of divine presence. The trembling (entromos) is the biblical response to theophany (Daniel 10:11; Revelation 1:17). He does not yet know who these men are beyond their miraculous non-escape; his trembling is the beginning of the awe that will issue in conversion.
30: kyrioi, ti me dei poiein hina sōthō ('Sirs, what must I do to be saved?'): the most direct articulation of the soteriological question in Acts. Dei (it is necessary) carries the weight of genuine urgency. Kyrioi ('sirs/lords') addresses Paul and Silas with respect; the word will be echoed in the answer's 'Lord Jesus' (Acts 16:31). The jailer asks as an individual; the answer encompasses his household — salvation's scope is simultaneously personal and communal.
31: pisteuson epi ton kyrion Iēsoun, kai sōthēsē sy kai ho oikos sou ('believe on the Lord Jesus and you will be saved — you and your household'): the Philippian kerygma in its most compressed form. Pisteuson epi (believe on/in) is trust directed toward a person, not assent to propositions. Ho oikos sou ('your household') extends the salvation promise to the household; the ancient household was a corporate social unit, and faith professed by the head opened the household to instruction and conversion (Acts 16:33-34). This is the basis of infant-baptism arguments and household-solidarity theology.
32: elalēsan autō ton logon tou kyriou syn pasin tois en tē oikia autou ('they spoke the word of the Lord to him and to all those in his household'): the conversion process is not instantaneous — it involves instruction (elalēsan, speaking/teaching). The word is delivered to the entire household, establishing that the conversion is both individual (the jailer's personal question) and communal (the household's instruction). Faith and baptism follow teaching; the Philippian church is founded on adequate instruction, not crisis-decision alone.
33: kai elou en tē nykti apo tōn plēgōn autōn ('he washed their wounds at that hour of the night'): the jailer washes their wounds — the very wounds inflicted by the city's civic machinery that his imprisonment was enforcing. His care for Paul and Silas is the concrete act that demonstrates the genuineness of his conversion before his baptism. Ebaptisthē autos kai hoi autou apantes parachrēma ('he and all his people were baptized immediately'): the immediacy of baptism in Acts reflects the apostolic practice of baptizing upon profession of faith, not after a lengthy catechesis.
34: hēgalliastō pepisteukas tō theō ('he was filled with joy because he had come to believe in God'): the joy of new faith is a Lukan signature (Luke 2:10; Acts 15:7, 10, 23-24; Acts 8:8, 39; Acts 13:52; Acts 16:34). The jailer's joy is explicitly connected to his belief: pepisteukas is the perfect tense — having believed with completed action in the present. The midnight scene ends with a meal hosted by the jailer for his prisoners — the table fellowship that marks conversion throughout Luke-Acts (Luke 5:29-32; Acts 15:23; Acts 19:6).
35: hēmeras de genomenēs ('when day came'): the narrative resumes in daylight, shifting from the intimate midnight scene of conversion to the civic-legal setting of the missionaries' release. Apelysan tous anthrōpous ekeinous ('release those men'): the magistrates' order uses the bare pronoun — 'those men' — without acknowledgment of the night's events. Their pragmatic order sets up Paul's challenge in Acts 16:37.
36: exelthe nyn kai poreuesthe en eirēnē ('go now and leave in peace'): the jailer's transmission of the release order uses the standard peace-dismissal. His communication is honest and complete; he has no reason to withhold the order. Paul's refusal to accept quiet release is not vindictiveness but strategic: a silent departure would leave the impression that the apostles were guilty of the charges, harmful to the reputation of the church they leave behind at Lydia's house.
37: Rhōmaious huparchontas ('being Romans/Roman citizens'): Paul's disclosure of Roman citizenship is strategically timed — withheld during the beating to establish the injustice, deployed after to create legal and diplomatic pressure. Akatakritous ('uncondemned/without trial'): the technical legal term for summary punishment without due process — a serious violation of Roman law (the Lex Valeria and Lex Porcia protected citizens from summary flogging). Paul's demand for a public acknowledgment protects the emerging church's civic reputation and sets a precedent for Christian legal standing.
38: ephobēthēsan de akousantes hoti Rhōmaioi eisin ('they were afraid when they heard that they were Romans'): the magistrates' fear reflects their legal exposure — flogging Roman citizens without trial was a punishable offense. The reversal of power — the imprisoned apostles now holding civic leverage over the magistrates — is Luke's ironic demonstration that the missionary movement operates within Roman law while transcending Roman authority. The empire that persecuted the apostles must apologize to them.
39: parakalountes ('entreating/appeasing'): the magistrates' escorting the apostles out is an act of public appeasement, not merely legal compliance. The verb parakaleō (exhort, beseech, comfort) is used throughout Acts for the community's encouragement; here it is deployed by civic officials forced into a supplicant position. Erōtōntes apelthein apo tēs poleōs ('requesting them to leave the city'): a polite expulsion — they want the problem resolved and the troublesome missionaries gone, but cannot force them.
40: eisēlthon pros tēn Lydian kai idontes parekalesan tous adelphous ('they went to Lydia's house, and having seen the brothers, they encouraged them'): Paul and Silas do not accept the magistrates' request to leave until they have returned to the church, reported what happened, and provided pastoral encouragement. Parekaloun ('encouraged') is the apostolic task — the same word as Barnabas's name. The church at Philippi will remain one of Paul's most beloved communities (Philippians 1:3-8; Acts 4:10-20). The 'we' passage ends here — Luke remains at Philippi.
